title: CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Opt-Out for Automated Decisioning Honored in Scoring API

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA/CPRA §1798.120 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.120 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.14: CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Opt-Out for Automated Decisioning Honored in Scoring API.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.4.14' running on sourcetype apigee:access and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: API gateway logs via HEC (Kong, Apigee, AWS API GW)
spl:
index=privacy sourcetype="consent:state" limit_automated_decisioning=true earliest=-30d
| eval eff=strptime(updated_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left consumer_id [
    search index=app sourcetype="apigee:access" api_product="risk_score_v2" earliest=-30d
    | rename developer_app_id as consumer_id
    | fields consumer_id, _time, response_code
  ]
| where _time>eff AND response_code=200
| stats count by consumer_id, api_product
| sort - count
description: Consumers may limit certain automated decisioning. Correlating scoring API calls with consent flags catches services still requesting model scores after an opt-out—reducing risk of statutory breaches tied to profiling choices.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CCPA/CPRA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes app, privacy and sourcetypes apigee:access, consent:state. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Align `consumer_id` keys across systems; (2) return 403 at gateway when possible—Splunk verifies drift; (3) redact payloads—use metadata only; (4) integrate with DSAR tooling; (5) legal defines `api_product` in-scope for “significant” decisioning. Indexes required: `index=app`, `index=privacy`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=apigee:access`, `sourcetype=consent:state`.
visualization: Table (violations), Timeline, Single value (calls post opt-out). Key fields: `api_product`, `consumer_id`, `eff`.
knownFalsePositives: Access requests from authorized regulatory inquiries, scheduled subject right campaigns, or outreach during privacy reviews.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: API gateway logs via HEC (Kong, Apigee, AWS API GW).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=app` `sourcetype="apigee:access"` (consumer_id, api_product, response_code, _time); `index=privacy` `sourcetype="consent:state"` (consumer_id, limit_automated_decisioning, updated_ts).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Align `consumer_id` keys across systems; (2) return 403 at gateway when possible—Splunk verifies drift; (3) redact payloads—use metadata only; (4) integrate with DSAR tooling; (5) legal defines `api_product` in-scope for “significant” decisioning.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=privacy sourcetype="consent:state" limit_automated_decisioning=true earliest=-30d
| eval eff=strptime(updated_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left consumer_id [
    search index=app sourcetype="apigee:access" api_product="risk_score_v2" earliest=-30d
    | rename developer_app_id as consumer_id
    | fields consumer_id, _time, response_code
  ]
| where _time>eff AND response_code=200
| stats count by consumer_id, api_product
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**CCPA/CPRA — Automated Decision Profiling — Opt-Out for Automated Decisioning Honored in Scoring API** — Consumers may limit certain automated decisioning. Correlating scoring API calls with consent flags catches services still requesting model scores after an opt-out—reducing risk of statutory breaches tied to profiling choices.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=app` `sourcetype="apigee:access"` (consumer_id, api_product, response_code, _time); `index=privacy` `sourcetype="consent:state"` (consumer_id, limit_automated_decisioning, updated_ts). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): API gateway logs via HEC (Kong, Apigee, AWS API GW). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: privacy; **sourcetype**: consent:state. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=privacy, sourcetype="consent:state", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **eff** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• Filters the current rows with `where _time>eff AND response_code=200` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates events by **consumer_id, api_product**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Output fields: api_product, consumer_id, eff.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (violations), Timeline, Single value (calls post opt-out).
grandmaExplanation: We consumers may limit certain automated decisioning.
